findings.description: The PDF has a complex annotated layout with a central sale tag surrounded by labeled callout arrows pointing to various sections, with explanatory text on both left and right margins. The HTML renders this as a flat, single-column linear document with no spatial relationship between annotations and tag content.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a bordered 'SALE TAG' in the center with arrows and annotations on left (e.g., 'Harvest Date; may be mo/dy/yr...', '90 Day tag retention notice') and right (e.g., 'Dealer Name, Address, & Certification Number', 'Type of shellfish...'). HTML renders all content linearly without any spatial grouping or visual indication of the annotation structure.

findings.description: The PDF contains arrows/lines connecting annotation labels to specific parts of the sale tag. These graphical elements are completely absent in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF has multiple arrow lines pointing from margin annotations to specific fields on the tag. HTML has no arrows or any visual indicators connecting explanatory text to the tag fields.

findings.description: The sale tag in the PDF is visually enclosed in a bordered box, clearly distinguishing it from the surrounding annotations. In the HTML, the tag content is not visually boxed or separated from the annotation text.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a clear rectangular border around the sale tag. HTML presents the tag content and annotations as a continuous flow without visual separation.

findings.description: Italic annotations in the PDF margin notes (explanatory descriptions) are rendered in italic in the HTML, but the visual distinction between tag content and annotation content is less clear without the spatial layout.
findings.evidence: PDF uses spatial separation to distinguish annotations from tag content. HTML relies only on italic formatting for annotations, which is preserved but less effective without layout context.

findings.description: The reading order differs between PDF and HTML. In the PDF, annotations on the left and right are spatially associated with specific tag fields. In the HTML, annotation descriptions are interspersed between tag fields in a top-to-bottom linear order, which changes how a reader encounters the information.
findings.evidence: PDF: A reader sees the tag as a whole with surrounding annotations. HTML: Annotation text like 'Dealer Name, Address, & Certification Number' and 'Certification Number of original shipper...' appear inline between tag fields, altering the reading experience.

findings.description: Nested <main> elements create invalid HTML structure. The outer <main> wraps everything, and an inner <main> is used for the content sections, which is semantically incorrect.
findings.evidence:
HTML has
containing

...

then another
element inside it.

findings.description: The PDF contains a visual diagram/example tag image with annotation arrows. No tag or alt text is present in the HTML for this visual element, though the content is rendered as structured text instead.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a bordered sale tag example with annotation callouts pointing to various fields. HTML renders this as structured text sections rather than as an image.
